preamble: The shift from the postalveolar fricative [ʃ] to a rhotic sound [r] is a form of rhotacism. It typically occurs in syllable-final positions or as part of a broader systemic weakening of sibilants, particularly in languages where the [ʃ] is already slightly retroflexed or apical in nature.

examples.note: Historical retroflex sibilants in some dialects have shifted toward rhotic or alveolar fricative realizations.
While the shift is usually r to sh, the phonetic boundary between these sounds in coda positions is highly fluid in many dialects.
